card.intuition: Pick the next unsorted element and slide it left into its correct position in the already-sorted prefix. The sorted prefix grows by one element per pass — like inserting a playing card into the correct position in a hand.
card.analogy: Sorting a hand of playing cards: you pick up one card at a time and slide it into the right spot among the cards already in your hand. The hand remains sorted after every insertion.
card.time_complexity: O(n²) worst case; O(n) best case on nearly-sorted data
when_to_use: Nearly-sorted arrays — O(n) in practice when few inversions exist
Small arrays (n < 20) — low constant factors make it fast in practice
Online sorting: elements arrive one at a time
gotchas: Stable sort: equal elements are never moved past each other
Inner while loop runs backward — shift elements right to make room for the key
Used as the base case in Timsort and Introsort for small subarrays

exercise.prompt: Implement insertion sort by shifting elements right until the correct position is found.
hints: After the while loop, j+1 is the correct insertion position — place the saved key there
exercise.test_code:
assert insertion_sort([12,11,13,5,6]) == [5,6,11,12,13]
assert insertion_sort([1]) == [1]
assert insertion_sort([]) == []
assert insertion_sort([3,2,1]) == [1,2,3]
assert insertion_sort([1,2,3]) == [1,2,3]
print('PASS')
